days.title: Meet Your Guitar
days.focus: Guitar anatomy, holding the guitar, holding the pick
days.intro: Welcome to day 1. Today you won't learn a single song — you'll learn to hold the guitar so comfortably that every future practice session feels easy. That comfort is what keeps people playing past week one.

steps.title: Learn the parts
steps.instructions: With the guitar on your lap, find and say out loud: headstock, tuning pegs, nut, neck, frets (the metal strips), body, bridge, and the 6 strings. Touch each part as you name it. Do this twice — you'll use these words every day from now on.

steps.title: Sitting position
steps.instructions: Sit on a chair without armrests, feet flat. Rest the guitar's waist on your right leg (left leg if you're left-handed), body against your stomach. Keep the neck angled slightly upward and your back straight. Let your strumming arm drape over the top of the body — the guitar should stay put even if you let go with both hands.

steps.title: Hold the pick
steps.instructions: Curl your index finger and lay the pick on its side, pointed tip aiming away from your palm. Press your thumb on top so only 2-3 mm of tip pokes out. Grip firm enough that the pick doesn't spin, loose enough that your hand stays relaxed. Take it out and re-grip it 5 times.

steps.title: Your first sounds
steps.instructions: Drag the pick slowly down across all 6 strings, from the thickest string to the thinnest. Don't worry about what it sounds like — no fingers on the neck yet. Do 10 slow strums, keeping your wrist loose and letting your arm do the moving.

youtube.title: Beginner Guide To Guitar Anatomy - Guitar Lesson Tutorial - Know Your Guitar

tips: If your shoulder or wrist aches, stop and reset your position — pain means something is off.
Keep the guitar somewhere visible. A guitar in its case is a guitar that doesn't get played.

days.title: Tune Up and Name Your Strings
days.focus: Tuning with an app and learning the open string names
days.intro: A guitar that's out of tune sounds bad no matter how well you play — and beginners often quit because they think they're the problem when it's the tuning. Today you learn to fix that in two minutes, every time you pick up the guitar.

steps.title: Warm-up strums
steps.instructions: Get into yesterday's sitting position and do 8 slow downstrums across all open strings. Check your pick grip: 2-3 mm of tip showing, relaxed hand.

steps.title: Learn the string names
steps.instructions: From thickest to thinnest, the strings are E, A, D, G, B, E. Remember it with 'Eddie Ate Dynamite, Good Bye Eddie.' Pick each string one at a time and say its name out loud. Run through all 6 strings four times.

steps.title: Tune with a tuner app
steps.instructions: Open a free tuner app (GuitarTuna or Fender Tune) and pick the thick E string, letting it ring. Turn its tuning peg slowly — if the app shows flat, tighten; if sharp, loosen. Small turns only, an eighth of a turn at a time. Work through all 6 strings, then go back and re-check the first two, since tuning one string can nudge the others.

steps.title: Quiz yourself
steps.instructions: Point at a random string, say its name, then pick it to hear it. Do 10 random strings. Finish with one slow strum across all 6 — that's what a guitar in tune sounds like.

youtube.title: How to Tune Your Guitar For Beginners

tips: Tune at the START of every practice from now on — it becomes a 2-minute habit.
New strings drift out of tune quickly for the first few days. That's normal.
Always tune UP to the note: if you overshoot, drop below and come back up.

days.title: Pick Technique: Open Strings in Rhythm
days.focus: Steady down-picking and moving the pick across strings
days.intro: Today your picking hand learns to keep time — the single most useful skill in music. Fifteen minutes of picking open strings sounds humble, but this is exactly how solid rhythm gets built.

steps.title: Tune and grip check
steps.instructions: Tune all 6 strings with your app. Re-grip your pick 3 times until it sits naturally: small tip out, relaxed thumb.

steps.title: Down-picks on the low E
steps.instructions: Count out loud '1, 2, 3, 4' at a slow, steady pace and pick the thick E string once per count, all downstrokes. Small motion — the pick only needs to travel about 2 cm. Do 8 rounds of 4 counts without stopping. If a pick catches or slips, you're digging in too deep; use just the tip.

steps.title: Cross the strings
steps.instructions: Now pick each open string 4 times, then move to the next: E-E-E-E, A-A-A-A, and so on from string 6 down to string 1. Keep counting '1, 2, 3, 4' and don't pause between strings. Watch your picking hand the first time through, then try a round without looking. Do 3 full trips across all 6 strings.

steps.title: Strum in rhythm
steps.instructions: Finish with full downstrums across all 6 strings, one strum per count: '1, 2, 3, 4.' Do 8 rounds. Keep the wrist loose — a strum is a brush, not a chop.

youtube.title: Picks, how to choose one and hold it (Guitar Lesson BC-107) Guitar for beginners, Getting started

tips: Counting OUT LOUD feels silly and works brilliantly. Do it.
Slow and even beats fast and sloppy — every single time.

days.title: Fretting Hand Basics: First Notes
days.focus: Thumb position, pressing near the fret, first fretted notes
days.intro: Today your left hand joins the party — you'll press strings down and play your first real notes. Your fingertips will feel tender by the end. That's the honest sign of progress every guitarist before you has felt.

steps.title: Tune up and warm up
steps.instructions: Tune with your app, then do 8 steady downstrums counting '1, 2, 3, 4' like yesterday.

steps.title: Thumb and finger position
steps.instructions: Put your thumb flat on the BACK of the neck, roughly behind the 2nd fret, pointing up. Curl your fingers over so the fingertips point down at the strings — like holding a ball. Don't let your palm grip the neck; there should be a small gap under it. Practice placing your index fingertip on the thinnest string and lifting off, 10 times.

steps.title: Your first fretted note
steps.instructions: Press the tip of your index finger on the thin E string just BEHIND the 1st fret (close to the metal, not on top of it). Press firmly and pick the string. If it buzzes, slide closer to the fret; if it's muted, press harder or check you're on the very tip. Play 10 clean notes, relaxing your hand between each one.

steps.title: Walk up the frets
steps.instructions: On the thin E string, play fret 1 with your index, fret 2 with your middle, fret 3 with your ring finger — one pick per note, counting steadily. Then do the same on the B string. Do 5 slow rounds on each string. Keep each finger just behind its fret.

youtube.title: Guitar Lesson 1 - Absolute Beginner? Start Here! [Free 10 Day Starter Course]

tips: Sore fingertips are normal for 2-3 weeks, then calluses form and it stops hurting.
Press just behind the fret, not on top of it — you'll need half the pressure.
Trim your fretting-hand nails short. Long nails make clean notes impossible.

days.title: Your First Riff: Seven Nation Army
days.focus: Playing a real riff on one string
days.intro: Five days in and you're already playing something people will recognize. Seven Nation Army lives on a single string, which makes it the perfect first riff — all your work this week comes together right here.

steps.title: Tune and fret walk warm-up
steps.instructions: Tune up, then do 3 rounds of yesterday's 1-2-3 fret walk on the thin E and B strings. Check your thumb is behind the neck and fingers are on their tips.

steps.title: Learn the notes one at a time
steps.instructions: The whole riff is on the A string (5th string, second thickest): frets 7, 7, 10, 7, 5, 3, 2. Use your index finger for every note and shift your whole hand along the neck — sliding between notes is allowed and sounds cool here. Play through the sequence one note at a time, pausing as long as you need before each note. Get through it 5 times, accuracy over speed.

steps.title: Slow loop
steps.instructions: Now play the riff start to finish without stopping between notes, at half the speed of the record. Loop it 5 times. If one jump keeps failing (usually 7 to 10), practice just those two notes back and forth 10 times, then rejoin the loop.

steps.title: Play with the video
steps.instructions: Play along with today's video at whatever speed you can hold. Don't stop when you fluff a note — keeping going is the real skill. End on one confident full run-through.

youtube.title: Play Seven Nation Army the EASY WAY - Your First Riff - Beginner Guitar Lesson

tips: Hum the riff while you play it — your fingers follow your ear.
Play it for someone today. First-riff pride is real fuel.

days.title: Two Strings, One Riff: String Crossing
days.focus: Crossing between strings while playing a riff
days.intro: Yesterday's riff lived on one string; today you learn to hop between two. String crossing is what separates plucking notes from actually playing guitar, and you're ready for it.

steps.title: Tune and riff warm-up
steps.instructions: Tune up, then play Seven Nation Army 3 times as your warm-up. Keep it slow and clean.

steps.title: String crossing drill
steps.instructions: Alternate downpicks between the open low E (6th) and open A (5th) strings: E, A, E, A, one note per count. The trick is small pick motion — hover close to both strings instead of swinging wide. Do 8 rounds of 4 counts. Then try it without looking at your picking hand for 4 more rounds.

steps.title: Learn the two-string riff
steps.instructions: This simplified Come As You Are riff uses the two thickest strings: low E frets 0, 1, 2, then A string fret 2, then A open, then back to low E fret 2, 1, 0. Use index for fret 1 and middle for fret 2. Learn it in two halves — first four notes, then the rest — playing each half 5 times before joining them.

steps.title: Loop it with the video
steps.instructions: Play the full riff on repeat, slowly and evenly, 5 times through. Then play along with today's video as best you can. Wobbles are fine; keep your count steady.

youtube.title: Nirvana - Come As You Are Guitar Lesson - Easy Beginner Riff

tips: Keep your pick within a couple of centimetres of the strings — big swings cause misses.
If you hit the wrong string, slow down 20%. Accuracy first, speed follows.

days.title: Week 1 Review: Lock It In
days.focus: Consolidating tuning, picking, and both riffs — plus your first recording
days.intro: You made it through week 1 — seven straight days is more than most people ever manage. Today is lighter: no new material, just polishing what you've got and capturing your first recording so future-you can hear how far you've come.

steps.title: Tune and free strum
steps.instructions: Tune all 6 strings, naming each one as you go (E-A-D-G-B-E). Then do 8 relaxed downstrums counting '1, 2, 3, 4.' Notice how much more natural the pick feels than on day 1.

steps.title: Riff medley
steps.instructions: Play Seven Nation Army 3 times, then the Come As You Are riff 3 times. Take a breath between runs. If one riff is clearly weaker, give it 2 extra runs — spend the time where it's needed.

steps.title: Play-along for fun
steps.instructions: Put on today's practice-routine video and play along with whatever sections you can — open strums, single notes, your riffs. This is play, not a test. Enjoy making noise on purpose.

steps.title: Record your 60 seconds
steps.instructions: Hit the Record button in the app and play for about 60 seconds: one run of each riff plus a few strums. Don't restart if you make mistakes — this recording is your honest week-1 snapshot. You'll listen back to these weekly and the improvement will genuinely surprise you.

youtube.title: JustinGuitar Lesson 1 Practice Routine | Guitar for Beginners

tips: Seven days in a row beats one three-hour Saturday session. You're doing it right.
Keep every weekly recording — by day 90 you'll have a highlight reel of progress.
